name: Elisabeth Elliot
source_summary: Elisabeth Elliot (1926–2015) was an American evangelical author and speaker. Following the death of her husband Jim Elliot, killed with four fellow missionaries by the Waodani people of Ecuador in January 1956, she went to live among the Waodani in 1958 with her daughter Valerie and Rachel Saint. The Waodani came to faith in Christ. She wrote Through Gates of Splendour, Shadow of the Almighty, and A Path Through Suffering, and hosted the radio programme Gateway to Joy.
adult_story:
Elisabeth Elliot was born in Brussels in 1926 to American missionary parents. She studied Greek at Wheaton College, where she met [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]]. On 8 January 1956, [[Jim|jim-elliot]] and four fellow missionaries were killed by the Waodani people of Ecuador — a tribe that had killed every outsider who approached them. Elisabeth was twenty-nine, with a ten-month-old daughter, Valerie.

She did not go home. In 1958, with Rachel Saint — sister of pilot Nate Saint, also killed — Elisabeth and Valerie went to live among the Waodani. For two years she lived in their community, learning their language. The tribe that had killed five missionaries came to faith in Christ, including several of the men who had carried out the killings.

She left Ecuador in 1963 and spent the rest of her long life writing and speaking. Through Gates of Splendour told the story of the five missionaries. Shadow of the Almighty preserved Jim's letters and journals. A Path Through Suffering drew on all she had lived.

She died in 2015. Her most quoted words: "God is God. Because he is God, he is worthy of my trust and obedience. I will find rest nowhere but in his will."
family_story:
Elisabeth Elliot grew up in an American missionary family and went to Ecuador with her husband [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]]. [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] is also on this site.

When Jim and four friends were killed by the Waodani people in 1956, Elisabeth was left alone with a tiny baby. She could have gone home. Instead, two years later, she went to live with the Waodani — the very people who had killed her husband.

She learned their language. The Waodani came to faith in Jesus, including some of the men who had done the killing.

Elisabeth spent the rest of her life writing and speaking about trusting God even through the worst suffering.

name: Brother Andrew
source_summary: Andrew van der Bijl (1928–2022), known as Brother Andrew, was a Dutch evangelical Christian and founder of Open Doors. Beginning in 1955 he made repeated journeys behind the Iron Curtain into Communist Eastern Europe, carrying Bibles and Christian literature, passing through border controls through what he described as answered prayer. His autobiography God's Smuggler (1967), co-written with John and Elizabeth Sherrill, sold more than ten million copies. He later worked to bring Bibles and the gospel to the Muslim world. He died in Harderwijk, Netherlands in September 2022, aged ninety-four.
adult_story:
Andrew van der Bijl was born in Sint Pancras, Netherlands in 1928 to a poor family. After a restless youth and a stint in the Dutch colonial army in Indonesia, he came to faith through a missionary tract and trained at a missionary college in Glasgow.

In 1955 he attended a communist youth festival in Warsaw and saw the reality of the persecuted church behind the Iron Curtain. He returned home determined to act. Over the following years he made repeated journeys into Poland, East Germany, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Yugoslavia, Bulgaria, and the Soviet Union, carrying Bibles hidden in a battered Volkswagen. At every border crossing he prayed: "Lord, you made blind eyes see. Now make seeing eyes blind — do not let the guards see what you do not want them to see." The Bibles crossed borders unseen.

He founded Open Doors in 1955 to support persecuted Christians worldwide, an organisation that today serves believers in over sixty countries. In later years he turned to the Middle East, meeting Hamas and Hezbollah leaders in order to bring the gospel.

He died in Harderwijk in September 2022, aged ninety-four. He wrote: "I believe in the power of the Bible more than any other weapon on earth." A man who spent his life putting God's Word into the hands of those who were dying to have it.
family_story:
Andrew van der Bijl grew up poor in the Netherlands. For a time he felt lost. Then he read a Christian leaflet and came to faith in Jesus.

He discovered that Christians in Communist Eastern Europe could not get Bibles — the government banned them. Andrew decided to take Bibles to them, hidden in his car. At every border crossing he prayed that God would make the guards unable to see the Bibles — and it worked.

He made dozens of trips and founded Open Doors to help persecuted Christians around the world.

He died in 2022, aged ninety-four, getting God's Word to people who risked everything to have it.

name: William Borden
source_summary: William Whiting Borden (1887–1913) was an American missionary candidate and philanthropist, heir to the Borden dairy fortune. At sixteen, during a round-the-world trip, he committed to missionary service. He gave away much of his inheritance, studied at Yale and Princeton Seminary, and sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic in preparation for working among Chinese Muslims in Kansu province. He contracted cerebrospinal meningitis in Cairo and died on 9 April 1913, aged twenty-five. The phrases 'No reserves. No retreat. No regrets.' were found written in his Bible.
adult_story:
William Borden was born in Chicago in 1887, heir to the Borden dairy fortune. At sixteen, his parents gave him a round-the-world trip. Confronted with poverty across the Middle East and Asia, he wrote home that he intended to give his life to missionary service. In his Bible he wrote two words: No reserves.

At Yale he was a gifted scholar and athlete who turned down lucrative prospects and gave away large portions of his inheritance to missions and Christian work. After Princeton Seminary he prepared to serve among Chinese Muslims in Kansu province. In his Bible: No retreat.

In 1912 he sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic on his way to China. He contracted cerebrospinal meningitis in Cairo and died on 9 April 1913, aged twenty-five, never having reached the mission field he had prepared for.

In his Bible, found after his death: No regrets.

Three phrases written across a decade, each at a different moment of cost — the testimony of a life held loosely from the beginning, released further at each step, and given completely at the last. Borden died in a hospital bed in Cairo. The phrase written at the end is what remains.
family_story:
William Borden was born in America in 1887, into a very wealthy family. When he was sixteen he travelled the world — and seeing so much poverty, he decided to give his life to missions. He wrote in his Bible: "No reserves."

At university he gave away much of his money to Christian work. He planned to be a missionary in China. He wrote: "No retreat."

In 1912 he sailed to Egypt to learn Arabic on his way to China. But he caught a serious illness and died in Cairo, aged just twenty-five, before ever reaching China.

In his Bible, found after his death, he had written at the end: "No regrets."

name: Mary Slessor
source_summary: Mary Mitchell Slessor (1848–1915) was a Scottish missionary to Nigeria. Born in Aberdeen and raised in poverty in Dundee, where she worked in a jute mill from age eleven, she served in Old Calabar from 1876 until her death. She moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, living among the Okoyong people and opposing the practice of killing twins. She rescued and adopted many children. In 1892 she became the first woman appointed to a judicial role in the British Empire, serving as magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong.
adult_story:
Mary Slessor was born in Aberdeen in 1848. Her father was an alcoholic shoemaker; the family moved to Dundee where Mary worked in a jute mill from age eleven, twelve-hour shifts, six days a week. Converted through the United Presbyterian Church and inspired by the death of [[David Livingstone|david-livingstone]], she offered herself for missionary service in West Africa.

She arrived in Old Calabar, Nigeria in 1876. She mastered the Efik language and moved progressively deeper inland than any previous missionary, eventually living among the Okoyong people far from any European presence. She wore local dress, went barefoot, and lived as her neighbours lived.

In the Calabar region, twins were believed to be cursed. One or both infants would be killed; the mother driven out. When Slessor heard the screaming she ran — sometimes barefoot through the undergrowth — to rescue the babies. She adopted many and raised them herself.

In 1892 she was appointed magistrate of the Court of Equity in Okoyong — the first woman to hold such a role in the British Empire. She died in Nigeria in 1915, having served for thirty-nine years.

A factory girl from Dundee, without formal education or social influence, who changed the culture of a region by refusing to look away from its cruelties.
family_story:
Mary Slessor grew up very poor in Scotland, working in a factory from the age of eleven. She became a Christian, inspired by [[David Livingstone|david-livingstone]], and went to Africa.

She went to Nigeria, learned the local language, and moved deep into the jungle — far from any other Europeans. She wore local clothes and went barefoot to be close to the people she served.

In that part of Nigeria, twins were believed to be cursed and were killed at birth. When Mary heard babies crying, she ran into the jungle to rescue them. She saved many, adopting some herself.

Mary served in Nigeria for thirty-nine years and never came home.

name: Adoniram Judson
source_summary: Adoniram Judson (1788–1850) was an American Baptist missionary, the first American Protestant foreign missionary, sent to Burma (Myanmar). He sailed for Burma with his wife Ann in 1812, converting from Congregationalism to Baptist conviction during the voyage. He was imprisoned for seventeen months during the Anglo-Burmese War of 1824–26. He produced the first Burmese Bible (1835) and a Burmese-English dictionary. He lost two wives and several children to illness. By his death in 1850 at sea, there were approximately 8,000 Burmese Christians and 100 churches.
adult_story:
Adoniram Judson was born in Massachusetts in 1788, the son of a minister. He sailed for Burma with his wife Ann in 1812 as one of America's first overseas missionaries, converting from Congregationalism to Baptist conviction during the voyage. His first Burmese convert was not baptised until 1819 — six years after arrival.

The Anglo-Burmese War of 1824 brought catastrophe. Judson was arrested as a suspected spy and imprisoned for seventeen months in Oung-pen-la — Death Prison — where prisoners were chained and suspended from bamboo poles at night. Ann walked to the prison daily, bribing guards for access and nursing their infant daughter Maria through the gates. Ann died in 1826 shortly after his release; Maria died weeks later.

Judson pressed on. He completed the first Burmese Bible in 1835 and compiled a Burmese-English dictionary of 25,000 entries. He lost two more wives in the field. He died at sea in 1850, having never returned to America. By then there were approximately 8,000 Burmese Christians and 100 churches.

"The future is as bright as the promises of God." A man who outlasted every loss not because he was immune to grief but because he had concluded that the gospel was owed to the Burmese regardless of what it cost him.
family_story:
Adoniram Judson sailed from America to Burma in 1812 — one of the first American overseas missionaries. For six years, no one became a Christian.

Then a war broke out and Judson was thrown into a terrible prison. His wife Ann walked to the prison every day to bring him food. When he was finally released, Ann died. Their baby daughter died soon after.

Judson stayed in Burma. He translated the whole Bible into Burmese so people could read it. By the time he died, there were thousands of Burmese Christians and one hundred churches.

He waited six years for his first convert — and never stopped trusting God through every loss.

name: Horatio Spafford
hymns: It Is Well with My Soul
source_summary: Horatio Gates Spafford (1828–1888) was an American lawyer, businessman, and Presbyterian Christian who was a close friend and financial supporter of D.L. Moody. He suffered major financial loss in the Great Chicago Fire of 1871. In November 1873 his four daughters drowned when the ocean liner Ville du Havre sank after a collision. His wife Anna survived. Sailing to meet her, he wrote the words of It Is Well with My Soul, which Philip Bliss set to music. Spafford subsequently lost a son to scarlet fever in 1880 and moved with his wife to Jerusalem in 1881, where he served the poor until his death in 1888.
adult_story:
Horatio Spafford was born in Troy, New York in 1828. A Chicago lawyer and businessman, he was a close friend and financial backer of the evangelist D.L. Moody and a man of active evangelical faith.

In 1871 the Great Chicago Fire consumed most of his real estate investments. In November 1873 he sent his wife Anna and their four daughters — Annie, Maggie, Bessie, and Tanetta — to France on the ocean liner Ville du Havre. He planned to follow shortly. The ship was struck by an iron sailing vessel and sank in twelve minutes. All four girls drowned. Anna survived and cabled him two words: "Saved alone."

Spafford sailed immediately to meet his wife. When the ship passed over the place where his daughters had drowned, he wrote the words of a hymn. [[Philip Bliss|philip-bliss]] — who is also on this site — set them to music: "When peace like a river attendeth my way, / When sorrows like sea billows roll; / Whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say, / It is well, it is well with my soul."

The hymn does not deny the grief. It claims something the grief cannot reach. Spafford later lost a son to scarlet fever. He died in Jerusalem in 1888, aged fifty-nine, having served the poor there for seven years.
family_story:
Horatio Spafford was an American lawyer and friend of the preacher D.L. Moody.

In 1873, his four daughters drowned when their ship sank crossing the Atlantic. His wife survived. She cabled him two words: "Saved alone."

Spafford sailed to meet her. As his ship crossed the same stretch of ocean, he wrote the words of a hymn that [[Philip Bliss|philip-bliss]] — also on this site — set to music:

"When sorrows like sea billows roll;
Whatever my lot, thou hast taught me to say,
It is well, it is well with my soul."

The hymn does not pretend the grief is gone. It says: even now, God is good.

name: John Wesley
footnote: Wesley taught the doctrine of 'entire sanctification' or 'Christian perfection' — the belief that a second work of grace can enable believers to love God and neighbour as their governing motive. This is distinct from sinless perfection and does not contradict salvation by grace through faith, but it is a significant Arminian distinctive that Reformed and Calvinist evangelicals would question. His Aldersgate conversion and the thrust of his preaching were firmly evangelical.
source_summary: John Wesley (1703–1791) was an English Anglican clergyman, theologian, and evangelist who founded the Methodist movement. Born the fifteenth child of Samuel and Susanna Wesley in Epworth, Lincolnshire, he studied at Oxford, was ordained an Anglican priest, and sailed to Georgia in 1735 as a missionary. He experienced evangelical conversion at a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street on 24 May 1738 — writing 'I felt my heart strangely warmed' and 'I felt I did trust in Christ, Christ alone for salvation.' He subsequently preached more than 40,000 sermons, travelled approximately 250,000 miles on horseback across Britain and Ireland, and founded Methodist societies that became a global movement. He wrote his final letter to William Wilberforce — urging him not to abandon the fight against slavery — a week before his death at age 87.
adult_story:
John Wesley was born in Epworth, Lincolnshire in 1703, the fifteenth child of Samuel and [[Susanna Wesley|susanna-wesley]]. He studied at Oxford, was ordained an Anglican priest, and sailed to Georgia in 1735 as a missionary — a venture that ended in failure and his return to England deeply uncertain about his own standing before God.

The turning point came on 24 May 1738. At a Moravian meeting in Aldersgate Street, London, Wesley heard Luther's preface to Romans read aloud. He wrote afterwards: “I felt my heart strangely warmed. I felt I did trust in Christ, Christ alone for salvation.” From that evening he preached the grace he had previously only theorised. With the open fields as his pulpit — following [[George Whitefield|george-whitefield]]’s example — he preached more than 40,000 sermons over fifty years, travelling 250,000 miles on horseback throughout Britain and Ireland. His Methodist societies became the seed of a global movement.

A week before his death in 1791 he wrote his final letter to [[William Wilberforce|william-wilberforce]], urging him not to abandon the fight against slavery. He died at eighty-seven, still trusting the Christ whose grace had warmed his heart fifty-three years before. For all his formidable organisation and energy, the thing that drove him was a single conviction: that a grace sufficient for John Wesley was sufficient for anyone.
family_story:
John Wesley was born in England in 1703, one of nineteen children. He became a minister and even sailed to America as a missionary — but he came home unsure he truly trusted God.

One evening in London in 1738, he heard someone read from the Bible about trusting Jesus alone. Something changed inside him. He wrote that his heart felt “strangely warmed” — and he knew Jesus had saved him.

After that, John spent fifty years on horseback all across Britain, preaching wherever people would listen. He gave more than 40,000 sermons. He never stopped pointing people to the grace that had changed him.

name: George Verwer
source_summary: George Verwer (1938–2023) was an American evangelical missionary and founder of Operation Mobilisation (OM). Born in New Jersey, he converted to faith in Christ at sixteen at a Billy Graham meeting in Madison Square Garden in 1955. He began distributing Gospel literature in Mexico in 1957 and formally founded OM in 1962. Under his leadership OM grew to thousands of workers in over a hundred countries. He launched OM's ship ministry — MV Logos (1970) and MV Doulos (1977) — using vessels as floating bookshops to distribute Christian literature at ports worldwide. He coined the term 'messiology' to describe his conviction that God works through imperfect people. He handed leadership to Peter Maiden in 2003 and died in London on 14 April 2023, aged 84.
adult_story:
George Verwer was born in New Jersey in 1938 and converted to faith in Christ at sixteen at a Billy Graham meeting in Madison Square Garden. The following year he began distributing Gospel literature in Mexico with a handful of friends; by 1962 he had formally founded Operation Mobilisation, which would grow to thousands of workers in more than a hundred countries.

Verwer’s two great convictions shaped everything OM became. The first was that the gospel was owed to the whole world, not a comfortable few. The second was what he called “messiology” — his term for the biblical truth that God works through fallible, stumbling people, not around them. He launched ship ministries to take the gospel to ports inaccessible to conventional mission teams: the MV Logos in 1970, the Doulos in 1977. He called himself “God’s Bungler” — not in false modesty but as theology.

He handed OM’s leadership over in 2003 but continued preaching, writing, and encouraging missionaries until shortly before his death in London on 14 April 2023, aged eighty-four. His legacy is hundreds of thousands of lives reached and a generation of workers from every continent who took seriously the same passage that had once gripped a sixteen-year-old in Madison Square Garden: Christ died for sins, once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring us to God.
family_story:
George Verwer was born in New Jersey in 1938. At sixteen, he went to a Billy Graham meeting and gave his life to Jesus. After that, one thing burned in him: the whole world needed to hear the gospel.

He started handing out Bibles in Mexico with a few friends. That small team grew into Operation Mobilisation — thousands of workers in over a hundred countries. He even bought ships to take the gospel to ports around the world.

George called himself “God’s Bungler” — because God uses imperfect people. He kept serving until he died in London in 2023, aged eighty-four.

name: Matthew Henry
source_summary: Matthew Henry (1662–1714) was a Welsh-born Nonconformist minister and biblical expositor, son of Philip Henry, who was ejected from the Church of England under the Act of Uniformity in 1662. Educated under Nonconformist tutors, Henry was ordained in 1687 as minister at Chester, where he served for twenty-five years, then moved to Mare Street Chapel, Hackney, London in 1712. He is best known for his six-volume Exposition of the Old and New Testaments, begun around 1704, the most widely read English Bible commentary ever written. He died in 1714 after falling from his horse near Nantwich, aged 51; the final volumes were completed by colleagues from his notes.
adult_story:
Matthew Henry was born in 1662 at Broad Oak Farm, Flintshire, Wales, the son of Philip Henry — a Nonconformist minister ejected from his parish that very year under the Act of Uniformity. From childhood he was formed by Scripture and by the cost of holding convictions under pressure.

Ordained in 1687, he ministered at Chester for twenty-five years, then moved to Hackney, London in 1712. He was known as a pastor who preached the whole counsel of God with warmth and loved his people faithfully.

His great work was his Exposition of the Old and New Testaments — a commentary on the entire Bible begun around 1704 and left unfinished at his death in 1714 when he fell from his horse near Nantwich. Colleagues completed the final volumes from his notes. It became the most widely read English Bible commentary ever written — not because of academic learning, but because of its devotional warmth and its consistent habit of pointing every passage to Christ.

His approach has endured for three centuries: Scripture speaks to the heart, not merely the intellect; every part of the Bible speaks of Christ; and the aim of Bible study is not to master the text but to be mastered by it. He wrote as a pastor who loved the souls before him.

The discovery that drove his life was simple: the grace of God in Christ is the beginning and end of all Scripture.
family_story:
Matthew Henry grew up in Wales in the 1660s, the son of a minister who lost his church for refusing to obey the king’s commands. From a boy, Matthew loved the Bible and learned what it cost to stand firm.

He became a minister himself and spent his life explaining God’s word to ordinary people. His greatest work was a huge commentary on the whole Bible — written clearly and warmly, always pointing the reader to Jesus. People have been reading it ever since, three hundred years later.

When Matthew opened any page of Scripture, he asked: where is Christ here? That question made his writing last.

name: Charles Haddon Spurgeon
source_summary: Charles Haddon Spurgeon (1834–1892) was an English Particular Baptist preacher, known as the ‘Prince of Preachers.’ Converted at fifteen in a Primitive Methodist chapel in Colchester, he was called to New Park Street Chapel, London at nineteen, and in 1861 moved to the newly built Metropolitan Tabernacle, seating six thousand. He preached to an estimated ten million people in person over his lifetime. He founded a Pastors’ College and an orphanage, and his sermons fill sixty-three volumes of the Metropolitan Tabernacle Pulpit. He battled depression and gout for much of his life. In 1887 he withdrew from the Baptist Union over its theological drift, an episode known as the Downgrade Controversy. He died in Menton, France, in January 1892, aged 57.
adult_story:
Charles Haddon Spurgeon was born in Kelvedon, Essex in 1834 and converted to faith in Christ at fifteen. During a snowstorm he ducked into a Primitive Methodist chapel in Colchester, where a stand-in preacher addressed the congregation on Isaiah 45:22 — “Look unto me, and be ye saved.” The words struck Spurgeon like a lance, and he looked, and he was saved.

By nineteen he was called to New Park Street Chapel, London. The congregation outgrew every building they could find; by 1861 the Metropolitan Tabernacle had been built, seating six thousand. He preached to an estimated ten million people in person over his lifetime. He founded a Pastors’ College, an orphanage, and wrote prolifically — his sermons filling sixty-three volumes.

He battled depression and gout for much of his life and was candid about it, writing that he had learned more of God’s grace through suffering than through any other school. In 1887 he withdrew from the Baptist Union over its drift toward theological liberalism, at considerable personal cost.

He died in Menton, France in January 1892. His message over forty years never changed: all are lost, Christ alone saves, and the grace that saves a sinner is the grace that keeps him to the end. He was not the message’s author — only its most tireless herald.
family_story:
Charles Spurgeon grew up in Essex, England. At fifteen, caught in a snowstorm, he ducked into a tiny chapel where a plain preacher pointed to Isaiah 45:22: “Look unto me, and be ye saved.” Charles looked — and was saved.

By nineteen he was preaching to huge crowds in London. A church was built that held six thousand people, and it was always full. He preached to more than ten million people in his lifetime.

Spurgeon sometimes battled deep sadness, but he said suffering taught him more about God’s grace than anything else. His whole life long, his message was the same: everyone is lost, and Christ alone saves.

name: David Brainerd
source_summary: David Brainerd (1718–1747) was an American Presbyterian minister and missionary to the Native Americans of New York, Pennsylvania and New Jersey. Orphaned at thirteen, he entered Yale College in 1739 during the First Great Awakening but was expelled in 1742 for a remark about a tutor, which barred him from licensed ministry. He was appointed by the Society in Scotland for Propagating Christian Knowledge and worked among the Delaware and Mahican peoples. In 1745 a remarkable revival broke out at Crossweeksung, New Jersey. Suffering from tuberculosis throughout his ministry, he died in October 1747, aged 29, at Jonathan Edwards’ home in Northampton, Massachusetts. Edwards edited and published Brainerd’s diary the following year; it has inspired generations of missionaries including William Carey and Jim Elliot.
adult_story:
David Brainerd was born in 1718 in Haddam, Connecticut, orphaned at thirteen, and entered Yale College in 1739 during the First Great Awakening. He was expelled in 1742 for a remark made in frustration about a tutor — a wound that barred him from licensed ministry and that he carried for the rest of his short life.

He became instead a missionary to the Delaware and Mahican peoples, appointed by the Society in Scotland for Propagating Christian Knowledge. He laboured in New York, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey — often in poverty, always ill with tuberculosis. He spent long nights in prayer in the forests, sometimes in such spiritual anguish that the snow around him melted.

In 1745 a remarkable revival broke out at Crossweeksung, New Jersey. Within months nearly a hundred Native Americans had come to faith. Brainerd preached through an interpreter but believed it was prayer, not his words, that opened hearts.

He died in October 1747, aged twenty-nine, at the home of Jonathan Edwards in Northampton, Massachusetts. Edwards edited his diary and published it the following year. That diary has moved readers ever since — most significantly [[William Carey|william-carey]] and [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]], who both counted Brainerd’s example among their chief reasons for going to the mission field.

He accomplished little the world counts great. He was spent for Christ, and that proved enough.
family_story:
David Brainerd was born in Connecticut in 1718 and lost both parents as a young child. For years he searched desperately for peace with God.

He eventually gave his life to Jesus and became a missionary to Native American peoples in New Jersey and Pennsylvania. He was often ill, cold, and alone — but he kept praying and preaching.

In 1745 many Native Americans came to faith through his ministry. He died in 1747, aged twenty-nine, worn out from tuberculosis.

His diary, written in his loneliest moments, has inspired hundreds of missionaries since — people like [[William Carey|william-carey]] and [[Jim Elliot|jim-elliot]] who read it and gave their own lives to reach the world with the gospel.

name: Gladys Aylward
source_summary: Gladys May Aylward (1902–1970) was a British evangelical missionary to China. Born in Edmonton, London, she worked as a parlour maid and felt called to China, but was rejected by the China Inland Mission training college. She saved her wages, purchased a train ticket via the Trans-Siberian Railway, and arrived in Yangcheng, Shanxi Province in 1930. She worked with veteran missionary Mrs Jeannie Lawson running an inn for muleteers, using the opportunity to share the gospel. She became a Chinese citizen in 1936 and served as a foot inspector for the local Mandarin. During the Japanese invasion she led a hundred orphan children over mountain passes to safety in Sian (Xi’an) in 1940, while herself seriously ill. She returned to Britain, then moved permanently to Taiwan, where she continued caring for orphans until her death on 3 January 1970.
adult_story:
Gladys Aylward was born in Edmonton, London in 1902 and worked as a parlour maid. She felt called to China but was rejected by the China Inland Mission training college on the grounds that her academic abilities were insufficient. She did not accept the rejection as final.

Saving every penny of her wages, she purchased a train ticket via the Trans-Siberian Railway and arrived in Yangcheng, Shanxi Province in 1930. She joined veteran missionary Jeannie Lawson, running an inn for muleteers — standing in the street to pull mule trains in by the bridle and, once the men were eating, telling them stories from the Bible. She eventually became a Chinese citizen, commissioned by the local Mandarin as a foot inspector, and built deep roots in the community.

When Japanese forces invaded and bombed Yangcheng in 1940, Gladys led one hundred orphan children on a two-week journey over mountain passes to safety in Sian (Xi’an). She was seriously ill throughout the crossing, but got every child there.

She later settled permanently in Taiwan, caring for orphaned children until her death in January 1970.

Rejected by the missions board, she went anyway. Dismissed as too ordinary, God used her extraordinarily. Her life is a testimony that calling comes from God — and God does not consult admissions panels.
family_story:
Gladys Aylward worked as a maid in London. She felt God was calling her to China, but when the mission board said she wasn’t clever enough, she didn’t give up. She saved her wages, bought a train ticket, and travelled to China alone.

In Yangcheng, Shanxi Province, she ran an inn and told Bible stories to travellers who stopped there. The local people loved her. She became a Chinese citizen.

When Japanese forces invaded, Gladys led one hundred orphan children on a two-week trek over mountains to safety. She was very ill — but she got every child there.

God called an ordinary maid to do extraordinary things — and she said yes.

name: C. T. Studd
source_summary: Charles Thomas Studd (1860–1931) was a British cricketer and missionary. The son of a wealthy Huntingdonshire landowner who converted to evangelical faith, Studd became a Cambridge cricket blue and England international. Converted at D. L. Moody’s Cambridge mission in 1882, he donated his entire inheritance of £29,000 to Christian causes and sailed for China with the ‘Cambridge Seven’ alongside Hudson Taylor’s China Inland Mission in 1885. He later served in India (1900–1906) and, aged fifty-two, founded the Heart of Africa Mission in 1913 (later WEC International), spending his final sixteen years in the Belgian Congo. He died at Ibambi in July 1931. His famous statement was: ‘If Jesus Christ be God and died for me, then no sacrifice can be too great for me to make for Him.’
adult_story:
Charles Thomas Studd was born in 1860, the son of a wealthy Huntingdonshire landowner recently converted to evangelical faith. Studd became one of England’s finest cricketers — a Cambridge blue and England international — and at twenty-one, entirely unconcerned about God.

The turning point came through D. L. Moody’s Cambridge mission in 1882. Studd was converted, and within three years had given his entire inheritance — £29,000 — to various Christian causes and sailed for China with the “Cambridge Seven” alongside [[Hudson Taylor|hudson-taylor]]’s China Inland Mission in 1885.

After six years in China he served in India (1900–1906) and then, at age fifty-two and already suffering from asthma, went to Africa. In 1913 he founded the Heart of Africa Mission — later WEC International — and spent his final sixteen years in the Belgian Congo. He died at Ibambi in July 1931.

He was not a simple man. He drove himself and others hard. But the conviction that drove him was theological, not temperamental: “If Jesus Christ be God and died for me, then no sacrifice can be too great for me to make for Him.”

Three continents. Total obedience. Everything poured out. He did not consider it heroism — he considered it the only logical response to a Saviour who had given everything.
family_story:
C. T. Studd was one of England’s best cricketers in the 1880s — famous, wealthy, and not thinking much about God.

Then he heard the preacher D. L. Moody speak — and was converted. He gave away his entire fortune, and sailed to China with a group of Cambridge students to serve as missionaries alongside [[Hudson Taylor|hudson-taylor]]. Later he went to India, and then, aged fifty-two and unwell, he went to Africa.

He died there in 1931.

His famous words were: “If Jesus Christ be God and died for me, then no sacrifice can be too great for me to make for Him.” He meant every word.

name: Thomas Barnardo
source_summary: Thomas John Barnardo (1845–1905) was an Irish-born Christian philanthropist and founder of homes for destitute children. Born in Dublin and converted through the Plymouth Brethren at seventeen, he came to London in 1866 to train as a doctor with the intention of going to China as a missionary. Encountering vast numbers of homeless children in the East End, he opened his first home for destitute boys in Stepney in 1870, with the notice ‘No destitute child ever refused admission.’ He founded a village home for girls at Barkingside in 1876 and eventually ran over ninety homes across Britain. From the founding of his first home in 1867 to his death in 1905, nearly 60,000 children had been cared for. His organisation continues to this day.
adult_story:
Thomas John Barnardo was born in Dublin in 1845 and converted to evangelical faith through the Plymouth Brethren at seventeen. Moved by news of the Taiping Rebellion, he came to London in 1866 intending to train as a doctor before sailing to China as a missionary. The children of the East End changed his plans.

On a winter evening, a boy called Jim Jarvis showed Barnardo a rooftop in Stepney where eleven homeless children slept on bare tiles in the cold. Barnardo could not look away. He began holding ragged school classes, then opened a mission room, and in 1870 opened his first home for destitute boys with the now-famous notice above the door: “No destitute child ever refused admission.”

He founded a village home for girls at Barkingside in 1876 and eventually ran more than ninety homes across Britain. By his death in September 1905, nearly 60,000 children had been cared for. The organisation bearing his name continues to this day.

He never reached China. But he understood his life’s work as no less a mission: the destitute children of Britain’s cities were as much a calling from God as any foreign field.

The gospel drove his philanthropy. Grace received expressed itself in mercy given — and the door was never closed.
family_story:
Thomas Barnardo grew up in Dublin and came to London as a young man planning to train as a doctor and go to China as a missionary.

One night a boy took him to a rooftop where eleven homeless children were sleeping in the cold. Barnardo couldn’t walk away. He opened a home for destitute children in the East End of London instead.

By the time he died in 1905, his homes had cared for more than 60,000 children. The famous notice above his door said: “No destitute child ever refused admission.”

He never reached China — but caring for London’s forgotten children was just as much a calling from God.

name: William Booth
source_summary: William Booth (1829–1912) was an English Methodist preacher who, along with his wife Catherine, founded the Salvation Army in 1865 (originally the East London Christian Mission, renamed 1878) and became its first General. Born in Sneinton, Nottingham, and converted to Methodism in his early teens, he became an independent evangelist after breaking with the denomination. He began preaching in the East End of London in 1865 to the poor and destitute. His 1890 book In Darkest England and the Way Out proposed a systematic plan to address poverty. By his death in 1912, the Salvation Army was operating in fifty-eight countries.
adult_story:
William Booth was born in 1829 in Sneinton, Nottingham, into poverty, and converted to Methodist faith in his early teens. Apprenticed to a pawnbroker, he could not escape the sight of what grinding poverty did to people. He became a Methodist minister, then broke with the denomination to become an independent evangelist.

In 1865 he began preaching in the East End of London — in tents, pubs, and the street — to people no respectable church would have sought. He called his mission the East London Christian Mission; in 1878 it was renamed the Salvation Army, with Booth as General and a military structure that gave formerly aimless men and women rank, discipline, and purpose.

His method was simple and radical: reach the person where they are, in their language, and meet their physical need alongside their spiritual one. His 1890 book In Darkest England and the Way Out proposed a systematic plan to address poverty through hostels, labour colonies, and skills training — not as a substitute for the gospel but as its immediate expression.

By his death in 1912, the Salvation Army was operating in fifty-eight countries. Booth never lost the conviction that drove him from the start: Christ died for every human being without exception, including those every other institution had written off.

He once told his son: “While women weep as they do now, I’ll fight. While men go to prison, in and out, in and out, I’ll fight. I’ll fight to the very end.”
family_story:
William Booth grew up poor in Nottingham in the 1830s and became a Christian in his teens. He could not stop thinking about the people no one else seemed to care about — the poor, the drunk, the forgotten.

He started preaching in the East End of London, where people were struggling terribly, and opened a mission to help them. In 1878 he named it the Salvation Army, giving it a military structure so that working people could serve God with discipline and purpose.

By the time he died in 1912, the Salvation Army was in fifty-eight countries. His message was always the same: Jesus died for every person without exception — including the ones everyone else had given up on.

name: Martin Luther
footnote: Luther’s later writings, especially On the Jews and Their Lies (1543), contain virulent antisemitic rhetoric that has no biblical warrant and has caused immense historical harm. These writings stand in stark contradiction to his theology of grace and represent a serious moral failure. They are condemned by Lutheran churches worldwide. His central theological contributions — sola fide, sola gratia, sola scriptura — remain foundational to Protestant Christianity.
hymns: A Mighty Fortress Is Our God
source_summary: Martin Luther (1483–1546) was a German Augustinian friar, priest, theologian, and professor who became the seminal figure of the Protestant Reformation. Born in Eisleben, Saxony, he entered an Augustinian monastery in 1505 after vowing during a thunderstorm to become a monk. Studying Paul’s letter to the Romans, he came to understand the righteousness of God as a gift given through faith in Christ rather than a standard to be achieved by human effort. On 31 October 1517 he posted ninety-five theses against the sale of indulgences; summoned before the Diet of Worms in 1521, he refused to recant unless shown by Scripture and reason that he was wrong. He translated the New Testament into German in eleven weeks (1522), married Katharina von Bora in 1525, and died in Eisleben in February 1546.
adult_story:
Martin Luther was born in 1483 in Eisleben, Saxony, the son of a copper miner who sent him to study law. During a violent thunderstorm in 1505, fearing sudden death, Luther vowed to become a monk. He entered an Augustinian monastery and pursued holiness with terrifying intensity — yet found only despair. However exhaustive his penance, he could not silence the voice that told him God was against him.

The breakthrough came through Scripture. Studying Paul’s letter to the Romans, he wrestled with the phrase the righteousness of God — and finally understood it not as the righteousness that condemns, but as the righteousness given freely to the sinner through faith in Christ. “I felt myself to be reborn and to have gone through open doors into paradise,” he wrote.

On 31 October 1517 he posted ninety-five theses against the sale of indulgences — the spark that became the Protestant Reformation. Summoned before the Diet of Worms in 1521, he refused to recant unless shown by Scripture and plain reason that he was wrong: “Here I stand; I can do no other.”

He translated the New Testament into German in eleven weeks (1522), giving ordinary people direct access to Scripture. He married Katharina von Bora in 1525 and modelled a new pattern of Christian domestic life.

He died in Eisleben in February 1546 — in the town where he was born. He left behind a Reformation built on a single rediscovery: by faith alone.
family_story:
Martin Luther was born in Germany in 1483. As a young man he became a monk, trying very hard to be good enough for God — but he never felt at peace.

Then, reading the Bible, he discovered the truth that changed everything: people are not made right with God by trying harder. God gives his righteousness as a free gift, through faith in Jesus Christ.

Luther told the world about this. He was put on trial and told to take it all back. He refused. He even translated the Bible into German so ordinary people could read it themselves.

His great discovery — that we are saved by faith alone, not by works — is still the foundation of the gospel today.

name: John Calvin
footnote: Calvin was directly involved in the trial and execution of Michael Servetus, who was burned at the stake in Geneva in 1553 for denying the Trinity. Calvin supported the execution and has been widely criticised for it. Capital punishment for heresy was widely accepted in that era and endorsed by other Reformers, but this does not diminish the gravity of the act. Calvin’s own writings on the episode reflect the moral blind spots of his time rather than his theology of grace.
source_summary: John Calvin (1509–1564) was a French theologian, pastor, and reformer who worked in Geneva during the Protestant Reformation. Born in Noyon, Picardy, he studied law before his conversion to Protestant faith around 1533. Forced to flee France, he settled in Geneva in 1536, where his Institutes of the Christian Religion — first published that year and expanded through several editions — became the most influential work of Protestant systematic theology after Luther. His theology centred on the absolute sovereignty of God and salvation entirely by grace through faith in Christ. He preached through entire books of the Bible systematically, founded the Geneva Academy in 1559, and corresponded extensively with Reformed churches across Europe. He died in Geneva in May 1564, aged 54.
adult_story:
John Calvin was born in Noyon, Picardy, France in 1509, and trained in law before what he called a “sudden conversion to teachableness” — a phrase that captures both his intellectual character and his sense that God had broken in unexpectedly. Forced to flee France after Protestant sympathies were linked to a controversial university address, he was persuaded to settle in Geneva in 1536.

His Institutes of the Christian Religion, first published that same year and expanded through several editions, became the most influential work of Protestant systematic theology. At its heart was the sovereign grace of God — that salvation is entirely God’s work, given freely through faith in Christ’s atoning death, received by none who deserve it, and secured by God’s own faithfulness to his own purposes.

In Geneva, Calvin preached through entire books of the Bible systematically, trained ministers for the French Reformed churches, corresponded with Reformers across Europe, and in 1559 founded the Geneva Academy — a seminary that shaped Reformed theology for generations.

He died in May 1564, aged fifty-four, worn out by relentless labour. His final instructions were that he be buried in an unmarked grave — a deliberate choice that God alone must be glorified.

His Institutes remain a theological landmark. His influence on Reformed and Presbyterian Christianity worldwide cannot be overstated.
family_story:
John Calvin was born in France in 1509 and trained to be a lawyer. Then God changed his heart, and he devoted his whole life to teaching what the Bible says about salvation.

Calvin believed that God saves people entirely by his grace — not because of anything people do, but because of what Christ has done. He preached through the whole Bible, trained ministers for churches across France and Europe, and wrote the Institutes of the Christian Religion, a famous book explaining Reformed theology.

He worked in Geneva, Switzerland, until he died in 1564, aged fifty-four. He spent himself completely in God’s service, believing that the goal of everything is for God alone to be glorified.

name: Joseph M. Scriven
hymns: What a Friend We Have in Jesus
source_summary: Joseph Medlicott Scriven (1819–1886) was an Irish-born Canadian poet and Plymouth Brethren believer, born in Banbridge, County Down, Ireland. Educated at Trinity College Dublin, he emigrated to Canada in 1844 after his fiancée drowned the night before their wedding. He settled in rural Ontario, where he taught school and devoted himself quietly to serving the poor. In 1855 he wrote a comforting poem for his gravely ill mother in Ireland; that poem was later set to music by Charles Crozat Converse and published as “What a Friend We Have in Jesus.” A second fiancée died of pneumonia in 1860. Scriven continued teaching, preaching, and giving away his possessions to those in need until his death by drowning in 1886, aged 66. He was buried at Pengally Cemetery near Bewdley, Ontario, beside his second fiancée.
adult_story:
Joseph Medlicott Scriven was born in 1819 in Banbridge, County Down, Ireland, the son of a Royal Marines officer. Educated at Trinity College Dublin, he seemed destined for a comfortable life. Instead, grief would mark his path. The night before his wedding in 1843, his fiancée drowned. Shattered, Scriven emigrated to Canada the following year, drawn by his Plymouth Brethren faith and a resolve to live wholly for Christ.

He settled in rural Ontario, teaching school, preaching, and devoting himself quietly to serving the poor — chopping wood for widows, helping those who could not pay, giving away what little he owned. He never sought prominence. He lived among the people he served.

In 1855, hearing that his mother in Ireland was gravely ill, Scriven wrote her a poem of comfort — privately, with no thought of publication. That poem became “What a Friend We Have in Jesus,” one of the most beloved hymns in the world. When a neighbour found the handwritten manuscript during his final illness and asked who had written it, Scriven replied: “The Lord and I did it between us.”

A second fiancée died of pneumonia before they could marry. He continued to serve, teach, and give until his own death by drowning in 1886, aged sixty-six. His gravestone carries the words: “Blessed are the pure in heart for they shall see God.”

Scriven found in Christ the friend the world could not be, and spent his life pointing others to the same.
family_story:
Joseph Scriven was born in Ireland in 1819. When his fiancée drowned the night before their wedding, it broke his heart. But instead of giving up, he trusted God and moved to Canada, where he spent his life quietly serving the poor — chopping wood for widows, giving away what little he owned.

One day he heard his mother was very ill. He wrote her a poem to comfort her — just for her, never meaning anyone else to see it. That poem became “What a Friend We Have in Jesus.” When asked who wrote it, Joseph said: “The Lord and I did it between us.”

He died in 1886, a humble man. But his hymn lives on, reminding millions that Jesus carries every care.

name: Robert Cleaver Chapman
source_summary: Robert Cleaver Chapman (1803–1902) was a British Open Brethren minister who served in Barnstaple, Devon for seventy years. Born in Elsinore, Denmark, to an English family, he gave up a legal career to enter ministry. A close associate of George Müller and Anthony Norris Groves, he was celebrated for extraordinary love, open-handed hospitality, and global missionary correspondence. Müller described him as the saintliest man he ever knew. Chapman lived to the age of 99.
adult_story:
Robert Cleaver Chapman was born in 1803 in Elsinore, Denmark, to an English merchant family. Converted in his early twenties, he trained as a solicitor, gave up a promising legal career, and in 1832 settled in Barnstaple, Devon, where he would minister for the next seventy years.

He became associated with the Open Brethren and was known across the evangelical world for a rare combination of doctrinal solidity and extraordinary love. [[George Müller|george-muller]], who knew him well, called him "the saintliest man I ever knew." Men travelled to Barnstaple simply to observe how he lived. His home was open to all — he fed the hungry, housed the homeless, and corresponded with missionaries across the globe.

Chapman lived to 99 and never retired from ministry. He was a poet, an evangelist, and a letter-writer whose counsel was sought by leaders across denominations. His maxim was that love is the greatest apologetic: "Let your life preach, then let your lips, if necessary, also speak."

His life pointed to the grace that produces unhurried, unboastful, enduring love — not a love manufactured by duty, but one rooted in a sight of Christ he had been cultivating for eight decades.
family_story:
Robert Chapman gave up being a lawyer to serve God in a small town in Devon, England. For seventy years he preached, prayed, fed the hungry, and helped the poor. [[George Müller|george-muller]] — who himself trusted God to feed thousands of orphans — said Robert was the most Christ-like man he had ever met.

Robert lived to be 99, always learning, always loving. He believed the best way to tell people about Jesus was simply to live like Jesus — and so he did, one quiet day at a time.

name: John Nelson Darby
footnote: Darby's doctrine that all Christendom was in a state of irreparable 'ruin' — requiring total separation from denominations — has been rejected by most evangelical traditions. His ecclesiology caused significant divisions, including the permanent rupture between the Open and Exclusive Brethren in 1848. These views should be assessed carefully.
source_summary: John Nelson Darby (1800–1882) was an Irish-born Christian leader who became a principal founder of the Plymouth Brethren movement and the originator of dispensationalism. Trained as a lawyer and briefly ordained in the Church of Ireland, he resigned his orders in 1828 and gathered believers around Scripture and the Lord's Table without clergy. He travelled ceaselessly through Britain, Europe, North America, and New Zealand, planting assemblies and translating the Bible into English, French, and German. His separatist ecclesiology caused a permanent split between the Open and Exclusive Brethren in 1848.
adult_story:
John Nelson Darby was born in London in 1800 and raised in Ireland. Trained as a lawyer, he was ordained in the Church of Ireland, then resigned his orders in 1828 — convinced that the institutional church had departed from New Testament patterns.

He became a key figure in the early Plymouth Brethren, gathering believers around Scripture and the Lord's Table without clergy or fixed liturgy. He travelled ceaselessly — Britain, France, Germany, North America, New Zealand — preaching and planting assemblies. His translations of the Bible into English, French, and German remain in use today.

Darby developed what became known as dispensationalist theology — a structured reading of biblical history that shaped much of later evangelical eschatology, particularly in North America. He believed Scripture was the church's only authority and that Christ's return was imminent.

His conviction that all denominations were irreparably corrupted led to painful divisions, including a rupture with [[George Müller|george-muller]] and the Open Brethren in 1848. His legacy is contested, but his devotion to Scripture and his missionary energy were genuine. He died in 1882 having translated the Bible into three languages and planted churches across the world.
family_story:
John Darby was an Irish lawyer who gave up his career to preach the Bible. He believed churches should be simple — no special clergy, just believers gathered around Scripture and the Lord's Supper, just as in the New Testament.

He spent his whole life travelling — to England, France, Germany, and across the Atlantic — starting small churches and teaching the Bible. He even translated the Bible into three different languages.

John had strong views that sometimes caused disagreements, but his love for the Bible and his belief that Jesus was coming soon drove him to work tirelessly for the gospel until he died at 82.

name: Robert Murray M'Cheyne
hymns: When This Passing World Is Done
source_summary: Robert Murray M'Cheyne (1813–1843) was a Scottish Presbyterian minister ordained as pastor of St. Peter's Church, Dundee, at the age of 23. He was known for his intense prayer life, personal holiness, and powerful preaching. In 1839 he joined a delegation to Palestine to survey Jewish mission prospects; during his absence revival broke out in his congregation. He died of typhus aged 29 while visiting sick parishioners. Andrew Bonar's Memoir and Remains became one of the most influential evangelical books of the 19th century.
adult_story:
Robert Murray M'Cheyne was born in Edinburgh in 1813 and converted in his late teens following the death of his older brother. He studied at Edinburgh University under Thomas Chalmers, was licensed to preach in 1835, and ordained as minister of St. Peter's, Dundee, in 1836 at the age of 23.

In the seven years of his ministry he became one of the most remarkable preachers Scotland had known. His power in the pulpit was widely attributed to his extraordinary prayer life and rigorous personal holiness — he is said to have risen at five each morning to pray before studying and preaching. In 1839 he joined a delegation to Palestine to survey the prospects for Jewish mission, and on his return found that revival had broken out among his congregation in his absence.

He died in 1843, aged 29, from typhus contracted while visiting sick parishioners during an epidemic. His friend Andrew Bonar's Memoir and Remains, published shortly after, became one of the most influential books in Scottish evangelical history.

M'Cheyne's life is a rebuke and a comfort — a rebuke to the idea that long years are needed for God's purposes, and a comfort that fruitfulness flows from proximity to Christ. "Lord, make me as holy as a pardoned sinner can be" was his prayer.
family_story:
Robert Murray M'Cheyne became a minister in Dundee, Scotland, when he was just 23 years old. He got up every morning at five o'clock to pray before doing anything else.

People came from far away to hear him preach — not because he was showy, but because they could tell he had been with God.

He died of typhus at the age of 29, having caught the illness while visiting sick people in his parish. He had only been a minister for seven years — but his life showed that what God does through us has nothing to do with how long we live, and everything to do with how close we walk with Christ.

name: Henry Martyn
source_summary: Henry Martyn (1781–1812) was a British Anglican missionary and Bible translator. Senior Wrangler at Cambridge in 1801, he sailed to India as a chaplain of the East India Company in 1805. He translated the New Testament into Urdu, supervised translations into Persian and Arabic, and travelled to Persia to revise the Persian text. He died of tuberculosis in Tocat, Turkey, in 1812, aged 31, while attempting to return home. He had given the New Testament to three language groups.
adult_story:
Henry Martyn was born in Truro, Cornwall, in 1781. He was a brilliant scholar — Senior Wrangler at Cambridge in 1801, the highest mathematical distinction of his generation — whose ambitions for academic fame were overturned by conversion and the example of [[William Carey|william-carey]].

Ordained and appointed as a chaplain of the East India Company, he sailed for India in 1805. He learned Urdu, Persian, and Arabic and threw himself into Bible translation. He completed a New Testament in Urdu (1809), supervised Persian and Arabic translations, and travelled to Persia for the sake of a revised Persian text — debating Islamic scholars in Shiraz on the claims of Christ.

His journals record a relentless conflict between spiritual longing and physical collapse. He suffered from tuberculosis throughout his missionary years and died in Tocat, Turkey, in 1812, aged 31, while attempting to return home.

"I cannot find it in my heart to grieve," he wrote, "for what Christ thinks worth dying for." He had given the New Testament to three language groups. His life was brief; his fruit enduring.
family_story:
Henry Martyn was one of the cleverest young men in all of England — a top student at Cambridge University. But after he became a Christian, he decided to use that mind for God, not for a comfortable career.

He sailed to India and then Persia, learning difficult languages so he could translate the Bible into Urdu, Persian, and Arabic — languages that millions of people spoke.

Henry was often very ill, but he kept working. He died at just 31, far from home, still trying to bring God's word to people who had never heard it. He had given three whole peoples the New Testament in their own language.

name: Pandita Ramabai
source_summary: Pandita Ramabai Sarasvati (1858–1922) was an Indian Christian scholar, social reformer, and Bible translator. Born into a high-caste Brahmin family, she became a celebrated Sanskrit scholar before converting to Christianity in 1883 in England. She founded the Mukti Mission near Pune as a refuge for child widows, famine victims, and women in poverty; the Mission became a centre of evangelical revival in 1905. She produced a Marathi translation of the Bible from the original Hebrew and Greek, completing it shortly before her death.
adult_story:
Pandita Ramabai was born in 1858 into a high-caste Brahmin family in India. Her father, a Sanskrit scholar, unusually taught her to read the sacred texts — a radical act in a culture that barred women from learning. Both parents died in famine, and she became celebrated as a Sanskrit scholar in Calcutta.

After years of searching in Hinduism for relief from its oppression of women, she encountered the Gospels. She was baptised as a Christian in 1883 in England, having come to believe that Christ alone offered dignity to the low-caste, the widow, and the outcast — a conviction tested and deepened throughout her life.

She founded the Mukti ("Salvation") Mission near Pune as a refuge for child widows, famine victims, and women in poverty. In 1905 Mukti became a centre of revival — prayer and conversion spreading through hundreds of women and girls.

She also produced a Marathi translation of the Bible from the original Hebrew and Greek, completing it shortly before her death in 1922.

Her life proclaimed what the gospel insists: that in Christ there is no Brahmin and no outcaste — only the redeemed.
family_story:
Pandita Ramabai was a famous Hindu scholar in India who could read Sanskrit, one of the hardest ancient languages. But when she read the Gospels, everything changed.

She learned that Jesus treated women with dignity, helped the poor, and welcomed outcasts — the very people her religion looked down on. She became a Christian and decided to spend her life doing the same.

She started a place called the Mukti Mission — "Mukti" means salvation — where hundreds of women and girls who had nowhere else to go could find safety, education, and the love of Christ. She even translated the whole Bible into the Marathi language.

She showed that Jesus truly is Lord over every culture and every caste.

name: Bakht Singh
source_summary: Bakht Singh Chabra (1903–2000) was an Indian Christian leader who founded more than two hundred assemblies across India, Sri Lanka, and beyond. Born into a Sikh family in Punjab, he converted to Christianity in Winnipeg, Canada, in 1929 after receiving a New Testament. He returned to India and planted churches on New Testament principles without Western denominational structures. His conventions drew tens of thousands across south India. He died in 2000 at the age of 96.
adult_story:
Bakht Singh was born in 1903 into a Sikh family in Punjab, India. He left for England to study engineering, then moved to Canada. Bitter, restless, and far from home, he was given a New Testament by a stranger in Winnipeg in 1929 and was converted after reading it through the night.

He returned to India and began preaching in a country resistant to Christianity as a colonial religion. He founded assemblies on simple New Testament principles — no Western denominational structures, no paid clergy, daily prayer, breaking of bread, and Scripture. He is estimated to have planted more than two hundred churches across India, Sri Lanka, and beyond.

His preaching drew vast crowds — thousands gathering across south India for conventions at which he would preach for hours. He was known for fasting, long night-watches of prayer, and direct, uncompromising proclamation of the cross.

He died in 2000, aged 96, still active in ministry. He had sought to show that Christianity was not the white man's religion imported from the West, but the faith of a risen Lord who belonged to every nation — including India.
family_story:
Bakht Singh was a Sikh boy from Punjab, India, who went to England and then Canada to study engineering. In Canada, someone gave him a New Testament. He read it all night — and by morning he had given his life to Jesus.

He went back to India and spent the rest of his long life planting churches — more than two hundred of them. He wanted to show that following Jesus wasn't a Western thing. Jesus belonged to India too.

Bakht Singh prayed for hours every day, fasted often, and preached to huge crowds. He lived to 96, still serving God right to the end.

name: John Stam
source_summary: John Stam (1907–1934) was an American missionary with the China Inland Mission who was martyred alongside his wife Betty by Communist soldiers in Anhui province, China, in December 1934. Born in Paterson, New Jersey, he sailed for China in 1932 and married Betty Scott in 1933. Their two-month-old daughter Helen Priscilla survived. His calm letter to CIM headquarters before his execution, expressing trust in God's sovereignty, became widely known. He was 27 years old.
adult_story:
John Stam was born in Paterson, New Jersey, in 1907, one of eleven children of a Dutch immigrant family. He came to faith through the Student Foreign Missions Fellowship and sailed for China in 1932 with the China Inland Mission. In 1933 he married Betty Scott, a fellow CIM missionary.

In December 1934 Communist soldiers captured John, Betty, and their two-month-old daughter Helen Priscilla in Jingde, Anhui province. John wrote a letter to CIM headquarters asking them not to spend effort or money on his ransom, trusting that God was in control. The following day he and Betty were marched through the streets and beheaded.

Helen was found alive two days later in their abandoned house, wrapped in blankets, left in the care of a local Christian. She survived.

John's life was brief but his testimony reached millions. His and Betty's letters and the account of their death were published immediately and circulated across the world, inspiring a generation of young missionaries. "Make me willing to be offered," he had prayed before sailing for China.

He was 27.
family_story:
John Stam went to China as a missionary with his wife Betty. They had a baby daughter named Helen who was only two months old.

When soldiers arrested them, John wrote a calm letter to his mission saying he trusted God whatever happened. The next day, John and Betty were killed for their faith.

Baby Helen was found safe, wrapped in blankets in their house. A local Chinese Christian family looked after her until she was rescued.

John was only 27. His life was short, but the story of his faith and courage inspired thousands of young people to become missionaries themselves.

name: Betty Stam
source_summary: Betty Scott Stam (1906–1934) was an American missionary with the China Inland Mission, born in Tsingtao (Qingdao), China, to missionary parents. She married John Stam in 1933. Captured by Communist soldiers in Anhui province in December 1934, she and John were beheaded on 8 December 1934; their infant daughter Helen Priscilla survived. Her pre-field prayer of total surrender to God's will became one of the most quoted missionary testimonies of the twentieth century. She was 28 years old.
adult_story:
Betty Scott Stam was born in 1906 in Tsingtao (Qingdao), China, where her parents served as missionaries. She grew up bilingual in English and Chinese, graduated from Wilson College in Pennsylvania, and sailed back to China with the China Inland Mission in 1931. She married [[John Stam|john-stam]] in 1933.

She had made her commitment clear before she went. As a student she had written: "Lord, I give up all my own plans and purposes, all my own desires and hopes, and accept Thy will for my life." Her letters from China showed a woman of steady faith, warm humour, and clear theological depth.

In December 1934 she and John were captured by Communist soldiers in Anhui province with their two-month-old daughter Helen Priscilla. On 8 December they were beheaded. Their baby was found alive.

Betty was 28. She and John had served in China for little more than a year as a married couple. Yet the account of their death, published immediately and distributed worldwide, became one of the most powerful missionary testimonies of the twentieth century — not because it ended in victory by human measure, but because it showed two young lives held loosely, offered freely, and not found wanting when the moment came.
family_story:
Betty Stam grew up in China, the daughter of missionaries, and went back as a missionary herself. She married [[John Stam|john-stam]], and they had a baby girl named Helen.

When soldiers arrested them, Betty was not surprised. She had written years before: "Lord, I give up all my own plans and accept Thy will for my life."

Betty and John were killed for their faith. Their baby daughter was found safe. Betty was only 28.

Her story showed that real faith is not just words we say, but a life we hand over to Jesus — and she had handed hers over long before the soldiers came.

name: Dietrich Bonhoeffer
footnote: Bonhoeffer's later prison writings (Letters and Papers from Prison) use language — 'religionless Christianity,' 'the world come of age' — that liberal theologians have interpreted in ways departing from orthodox Christianity. Most evangelical scholars regard his core convictions, especially as expressed in The Cost of Discipleship and Life Together, as soundly orthodox. Readers should approach his later writings with discernment.
source_summary: Dietrich Bonhoeffer (1906–1945) was a German Lutheran pastor and theologian who actively resisted the Nazi regime. A founding member of the Confessing Church, he directed the illegal Finkenwalde seminary from 1935 until the Gestapo closed it in 1937. He authored The Cost of Discipleship and Life Together during this period. Arrested in April 1943 for his involvement in resistance activities, he was executed at Flossenbürg concentration camp on 9 April 1945, three weeks before Germany's surrender. His later prison writings remain theologically contested among evangelical scholars.
adult_story:
Dietrich Bonhoeffer was born in Breslau in 1906, the son of a psychiatrist. He studied theology in Berlin and at Union Theological Seminary in New York, and was ordained as a Lutheran pastor. When Hitler came to power in 1933, Bonhoeffer immediately recognised the threat and joined the Confessing Church — the movement within German Protestantism that resisted the Nazi-aligned "German Christians."

He directed an illegal seminary at Finkenwalde from 1935 until the Gestapo closed it in 1937. His books The Cost of Discipleship and Life Together were written in this period. In them he argued that "cheap grace" — forgiveness without repentance, faith without obedience — was a corruption of the gospel; true discipleship meant taking up the cross in the concrete circumstances of one's life.

He was arrested in April 1943 and executed at Flossenbürg concentration camp on 9 April 1945, three weeks before Germany's surrender. The camp doctor who witnessed his death wrote that he had never seen anyone die so entirely submissive to the will of God.

"When Christ calls a man," he wrote, "he bids him come and die."
family_story:
Dietrich Bonhoeffer was a German pastor who refused to go along with Adolf Hitler's evil government. When Hitler tried to take over the church, Bonhoeffer stood against him and helped run an underground school to train pastors who were loyal to Christ, not to the Nazis.

He was arrested and put in prison. On 9 April 1945 — just three weeks before the war ended — he was executed.

A doctor who watched him die said he had never seen anyone face death with such calm and trust in God.

Dietrich believed that following Jesus isn't cheap or easy — it costs everything. And he gave everything.

name: Joni Eareckson Tada
source_summary: Joni Eareckson Tada (born 1949) is an American Christian author, artist, and disability advocate. At age 17 she became a quadriplegic following a diving accident in the Chesapeake Bay in July 1967. After a period of intense struggle with depression and despair, she emerged with a deepened Christian faith. She founded Joni and Friends in 1979, a ministry serving people with disabilities and their families worldwide. She has authored over fifty books and paints holding a brush in her mouth. She has also survived breast cancer and continues in ministry.
adult_story:
Joni Eareckson Tada was born in 1949 in Baltimore, Maryland. In July 1967, at the age of 17, she dived into the Chesapeake Bay and struck the bottom — breaking her neck and leaving her a quadriplegic.

The years that followed were among the darkest of her life. She wrestled with depression, rage, and a desire to die. She was sustained by friends who refused to leave, by Scripture, and by a slow, painful re-engagement with a God whose purposes she could not yet see. She emerged not with easy answers, but with a conviction — deepened across six decades — that suffering is not evidence of God's absence but the terrain on which he is most clearly known.

She founded Joni and Friends in 1979, a ministry now active worldwide serving people with disabilities and their families. She has authored over fifty books, recorded music, and painted with a brush held in her mouth. She has also endured breast cancer and chronic pain.

She has said publicly that heaven is more real to her than her wheelchair — not as an escape from suffering, but as the resolution of a story she believes God is still writing.
family_story:
When Joni Eareckson Tada was 17 years old, she dived into a lake, hit the bottom, and broke her neck. She has been in a wheelchair ever since, unable to use her hands.

At first she was angry and heartbroken. But slowly, through reading the Bible and praying, she came to trust that God had not abandoned her — and that her life still had meaning.

She started Joni and Friends, a ministry helping people with disabilities worldwide. She paints pictures holding a brush in her mouth, and has written over fifty books.

Joni says heaven feels more real than her wheelchair — not because she wants to escape, but because she believes God is writing a story that will one day make sense of everything.

name: Dwight L. Moody
source_summary: Dwight L. Moody (1837–1899) was an American non-denominational evangelist and educator. Born in poverty in Northfield, Massachusetts, his father died when he was four. He converted at eighteen in Boston through the witness of his Sunday school teacher Edward Kimball. Moving to Chicago, he founded what became Moody Church and, with singer Ira Sankey, conducted mass evangelistic campaigns across Britain and North America in the 1870s and 1880s. He founded Northfield Schools and in 1886 established what became Moody Bible Institute. It is estimated he preached to more than one hundred million people in his lifetime.
adult_story:
Dwight Lyman Moody was born in 1837 in Northfield, Massachusetts, the sixth of nine children. His father died when Dwight was four, leaving the family in poverty. He had little formal education and moved to Boston at seventeen to work in a shoe store.

His employer's condition was that he attend Sunday school. It was there that his teacher Edward Kimball sought him out in the back room of the shop in 1855 and asked if he was willing to give his life to Christ. Moody was. It was, as he later described it, the day everything changed.

He moved to Chicago, prospered in business, and might have remained prosperous — but in 1860 he gave it up for full-time ministry among the poor of the city. He built a Sunday school in the slums that grew into what became Moody Church. He partnered with singer Ira Sankey, and together they conducted evangelistic campaigns across Britain and North America through the 1870s and 1880s that drew hundreds of thousands.

He founded Northfield schools in Massachusetts, and in 1886 established the school that became Moody Bible Institute — training ordinary men and women for ministry without requiring the formal education he himself had never received.

He died in 1899. It is estimated he preached to more than one hundred million people — each one brought face to face with a God who, as Moody put it, is not angry but loves them.
family_story:
Dwight L. Moody grew up very poor in Massachusetts after his father died when he was just four years old. At seventeen he moved to Boston to work in a shoe shop.

His boss made him go to Sunday school. One day his teacher came to the shop and asked Dwight in the back room if he was ready to give his life to Jesus. He said yes — and nothing was ever the same.

Moody moved to Chicago, gave up a good business career, and spent the rest of his life telling people that God loved them. He preached to huge crowds across America and Britain, and started a school to train preachers who couldn't afford a university education.

He showed that God uses ordinary people to do extraordinary things.
