name: W. E. Vine
source_summary: William Edwy Vine (1873–1949) was a British Open Brethren Bible teacher and author based in Bath, Somerset. He studied at University College, Bristol, and devoted his career to teaching, preaching, and writing in Brethren assemblies. His principal work, Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words, published in four volumes between 1939 and 1941, traces the Greek roots of New Testament vocabulary in plain English, making original-language scholarship accessible to preachers and lay readers without formal Greek training.
adult_story:
William Edwy Vine was born in 1873 in Somerset and educated at University College, Bristol. He spent his entire adult life in Open Brethren assemblies — teaching, preaching, and writing in the conviction that Scripture, rightly understood, was the church's sufficient guide.

He is best known for Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words, published in four volumes between 1939 and 1941. The Dictionary traces every significant word in the Greek New Testament back to its root, explaining its range of meaning in careful, plain English. For the first time, preachers and teachers without formal Greek training could access the depth of the original text — the difference between agapē and philia, between justification and righteousness, between knowledge that merely informs and wisdom that transforms.

Vine wrote it in the conviction that the words of Scripture were God-breathed in every detail, and that careful attention to them was an act of worship as much as an act of study. He completed the work in his late sixties, seeing it through to publication near the end of his life.

His name is not widely celebrated. But Vine's Dictionary sits on the shelves of preachers, teachers, and careful Bible readers around the world — a quiet instrument, still doing what he intended: placing the wealth of the New Testament's words within reach of those who love them.
family_story:
W.E. Vine was an English Bible teacher who spent his life helping ordinary Christians understand the New Testament more clearly.

His most famous book is Vine's Expository Dictionary of New Testament Words. It explains what the important Greek words in the New Testament really mean — because the New Testament was written in Greek, and sometimes a translation can't capture the full depth of the original.

He believed every Christian, not just scholars, deserved to understand what God's word actually said. Thousands of pastors and teachers all over the world still use his Dictionary today.

He showed that careful attention to Scripture is one of the most important ways we can serve the church — and that you don't have to be famous to leave something lasting.

name: James Strong
source_summary: James Strong (1822–1894) was an American Methodist biblical scholar and professor of exegetical theology at Drew Theological Seminary, New Jersey. After more than thirty years of work with a team of scholars, he published Strong's Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible in 1890 — the most comprehensive concordance of the King James Bible ever compiled. The concordance cross-references every word with a Hebrew and Greek lexicon using a numbering system ('Strong's numbers') that remains the standard reference in biblical word study more than 130 years later.
adult_story:
James Strong was born in New York City in 1822. After studies in classics and theology he served for many years as professor of exegetical theology at Drew Theological Seminary in New Jersey, and as a Methodist churchman devoted to the careful study of Scripture.

In 1890, after more than thirty years of labour, he published his Exhaustive Concordance of the Bible. Every word in the King James Bible is indexed, and each entry is linked by number to its original Hebrew or Greek term in a companion lexicon. The work was compiled by hand, before computers — a feat of sustained scholarly patience by Strong and his team that took decades to complete.

Strong's numbers, as they came to be known, became a standard reference tool that remains in daily use more than 130 years later. A preacher with no knowledge of biblical languages can look up any English word, find its number, and trace it back to its root in the original text — a doorway Strong spent his life building and leaving open.

He died in 1894. The Concordance outlasted him and is still published today, probably the most widely used Bible study reference in the English-speaking world.
family_story:
James Strong was an American professor who spent over thirty years building one of the most useful Bible study tools ever made.

His book — Strong's Exhaustive Concordance — lists every single word in the King James Bible, tells you exactly where to find it, and links it to the original Hebrew or Greek word it was translated from. He did it all by hand, with a team of helpers, with no computers.

His numbering system — Strong's numbers — is still used in Bible study apps and books today, more than 130 years later.

He showed that loving the Bible means being willing to work hard to understand it — and that careful, patient scholarship is its own kind of service to the church.

name: Alexander Cruden
source_summary: Alexander Cruden (1699–1770) was a Scottish Presbyterian bookseller, proofreader, and biblical scholar who lived and worked in London. He compiled his Complete Concordance to the Holy Scriptures largely alone, publishing the first edition in 1737 — the first major concordance of the entire English Bible. He revised it twice more; the 1769 edition remains in print. Cruden experienced significant personal instability, including three commitments to private asylums, but was earnest in his Calvinist Presbyterian faith and devoted to making Scripture accessible to ordinary readers.
adult_story:
Alexander Cruden was born in Aberdeen in 1699, educated at Marischal College, and came to London in his twenties where he worked as a bookseller and proofreader. He was a committed Calvinist Presbyterian whose personal life was marked by grief, loneliness, and several breakdowns — episodes that led to his brief commitments to private asylums, events his enemies mocked and his friends mourned.

In 1737 he published the first edition of his Complete Concordance to the Holy Scriptures, having compiled it largely alone over years of painstaking work. It was the first major concordance of the entire English Bible: every significant word indexed by chapter and verse, so that any reader could locate any passage by a word they remembered. He dedicated it to Queen Caroline. He revised and expanded it in 1761 and 1769; the final edition remains in print.

In his later years he styled himself "Alexander the Corrector" and mounted a self-appointed campaign for national moral reform. He was eccentric, sometimes erratic — but he was earnest. He prayed over every page of his Concordance and regarded his life's work as a gift to the church.

His Concordance outlasted his eccentricities. For nearly three centuries it has been placing the words of Scripture within reach of those who need to find them.
family_story:
Alexander Cruden was a Scottish bookseller in London who spent years doing one of the most useful things anyone has ever done for Bible readers.

He compiled the first big concordance of the whole English Bible — a book that lists every important word in Scripture and tells you exactly where to find it. He did most of the work himself, alone, over many years.

His life had some very hard times — he struggled with his health and with loneliness, and people sometimes mocked him. But he kept working, because he believed God's word deserved to be findable by anyone.

The concordance he made — Cruden's Concordance — has been in print for nearly 300 years. Millions of people have used it to find the passages they needed.

name: William MacDonald
source_summary: William MacDonald (1917–2007) was an American Plymouth Brethren teacher and author. He served as president of Emmaus Bible College in Oak Park, Illinois (1959–1965) before pursuing a wider Bible-teaching ministry across the United States, Europe, and Asia. He published more than eighty books, above all the Believer's Bible Commentary (1989), a single-volume exposition of every book of Scripture widely used among evangelical Christians.
adult_story:
William MacDonald was born on 7 January 1917 in Leominster, Massachusetts. After earning degrees from Tufts College and Harvard Business School, he worked as an investment analyst at the First National Bank of Boston — but commerce gave way to conviction. He served in the Navy during the Second World War, and afterwards gave himself entirely to Bible teaching through the Plymouth Brethren.

From 1946 he served on the faculty of Emmaus Bible School in Oak Park, Illinois, becoming its president in 1959. After stepping down in 1965, he spent years teaching in the United States, Europe, and Asia, then joined the Discipleship Intern Training Program in California, which he served until 1996.

MacDonald published more than eighty books, but his lasting monument is the *Believer's Bible Commentary* (1989) — a single-volume exposition of every book of Scripture, written in plain and accessible prose. Where figures such as [[William E. Vine|william-edwy-vine]] had mapped the language of the New Testament, MacDonald mapped the whole landscape of the Bible for the ordinary believer. His *True Discipleship* pressed the saved believer beyond nominal religion into whole-hearted allegiance — not confusing the cost of discipleship with the free gift of salvation, but refusing to accept that a faith with no fruit in one's life was saving faith at all.

He died on 25 December 2007, Christmas Day, having spent sixty years making the Word of Christ dwell richly in whoever would read.
family_story:
William MacDonald was an American teacher who believed the Bible was the most important book ever written — and spent his life helping ordinary people understand it.

After studying at some of America's most famous universities, he gave up a career in banking to teach God's Word full-time. He led Emmaus Bible College and wrote more than eighty books.

His biggest book, the *Believer's Bible Commentary*, goes through every book of the Bible and explains what it means. Millions of Christians have used it to understand Scripture better.

Bill MacDonald also wrote about what it truly means to follow Jesus — not just in words, but with your whole life. He spent sixty years helping others trust in God's grace.

name: H.A. Ironside
source_summary: Henry Allan 'Harry' Ironside (1876–1951) was a Canadian-American Bible teacher, preacher, and pastor who served Moody Church in Chicago from 1929 to 1948. Born in Toronto and raised in Los Angeles, he preached his first sermons as a child and was converted at thirteen. He served in the Salvation Army as a teenager, later joined the Plymouth Brethren, and from 1916 to 1929 preached nearly 7,000 sermons to over 1.25 million listeners. He published more than 100 books and was known as 'the Archbishop of Fundamentalism.'
adult_story:
Henry Allan Ironside was born in Toronto in 1876 and raised by his widowed mother after his father died of typhoid when Harry was just two. The family moved to Los Angeles in 1886, and at eleven years old Harry gathered neighbourhood children under a canvas tent and taught them the Bible himself, averaging sixty each session. In 1888 he heard [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]] preach to eight thousand people — and resolved to do the same. After months wrestling with whether he was truly born again, he trusted Christ at thirteen. 'I rested on the Word of God and confessed Christ as my Saviour,' he said.

By sixteen he was a full-time Salvation Army preacher delivering four hundred sermons a year. He later joined the Plymouth Brethren, moving in the same world as [[George Müller|george-muller]], and from 1916 preached nearly seven thousand sermons to more than a million and a quarter listeners.

In 1929 he was called to pastor Moody Church in Chicago — the church built in the tradition of [[Dwight L. Moody|dwight-l-moody]] — where he served for nineteen years, filling its four-thousand-seat auditorium Sunday after Sunday. He served as president of the Africa Inland Mission from 1942.

Ironside published more than a hundred books, commentaries on virtually every book of the Bible, written with the clarity of a man who had left school after the eighth grade but never stopped learning from Scripture. He died in New Zealand in January 1951, still mid-tour, still bearing witness.
family_story:
Harry Ironside grew up without a father — his dad died when Harry was just two. When his family moved to Los Angeles, eleven-year-old Harry could not find a Sunday school. So he started his own, sewing old sacks into a tent — and up to sixty children came each week.

At thirteen, Harry trusted in Jesus after months of wondering whether he was truly saved. From that day he never stopped telling others about Christ. By sixteen he was a full-time preacher.

Harry eventually pastored Moody Church in Chicago — a building that held four thousand people — and wrote more than a hundred Bible books. He left school after eighth grade, but he never stopped learning from God's Word.

name: John Wycliffe
source_summary: John Wycliffe (c.1328–1384) was an English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, called the ‘Morning Star of the Reformation.’ He challenged papal authority, denounced clerical wealth, and championed Scripture as the supreme authority of the church over a century before Luther. Rector of Lutterworth from 1374, he initiated the first translation of the Bible into Middle English. In 1377 he was summoned before the Archbishop of Canterbury at St Paul’s Cathedral in London; the proceedings ended in disorder. His writings directly shaped Jan Hus, and through Hus the Protestant Reformation. The Council of Constance condemned him posthumously in 1415; in 1428 his bones were exhumed and burned, his ashes cast into the River Swift at Lutterworth.
adult_story:
John Wycliffe was born around 1328 in Yorkshire and became one of Oxford’s foremost scholars — a philosopher, theologian, and doctor of divinity. He became rector of Lutterworth in Leicestershire in 1374, a post he held until his death.

He is called the “Morning Star of the Reformation” for the convictions he held a century before Luther: that Scripture holds supreme authority over church and pope, that salvation rests on God’s grace and election rather than on the church’s sacramental system, and that every person deserves to hear God’s Word in their own language. He challenged the doctrine of transubstantiation and denounced clerical corruption openly. In 1377 he was summoned before the Archbishop of Canterbury at St Paul’s Cathedral in London — the proceedings collapsed in disorder, and he returned to Lutterworth unmolested.

His most enduring legacy was to begin translating the Bible into Middle English. His writings directly shaped [[John Hus|john-hus]], and through Hus the whole Protestant Reformation. [[William Tyndale|william-tyndale]], who completed the first printed English New Testament, followed a path Wycliffe had opened.

The Council of Constance condemned him posthumously in 1415. In 1428 his bones were exhumed and burned, his ashes cast into the River Swift. As the river carried his ashes to the sea, so his doctrine spread across the world.
family_story:
John Wycliffe lived in England about 650 years ago. He was a professor at Oxford University and a village priest in Lutterworth.

John believed the Bible — not the Pope — should be the highest authority. He translated it into English so that ordinary people could read God’s Word for the first time.

Church leaders were furious. He was summoned to London to stand trial — but stood firm and came home safely. After he died, his enemies dug up his bones and burned them, throwing his ashes into the River Swift.

But his ideas spread like his ashes across rivers and seas — and lit the fire of the Reformation.

name: Charlotte Elliott
hymns: Just As I Am, Without One Plea
My God and Father While I Stray
source_summary: Charlotte Elliott (1789–1871) was an English evangelical Anglican hymn writer born in Brighton, granddaughter of Henry Venn of the Clapham Sect. After a spiritually empty early life she came to faith in 1822 through Swiss evangelist César Malan, who told her to 'come just as you are.' From 1829 she suffered chronic illness and spent most of her life confined to her room, yet edited The Invalid's Hymn Book and wrote approximately 150 hymns. Her most famous, 'Just As I Am, Without One Plea' (1836), became the defining invitation hymn of evangelical crusades and was used by Billy Graham at virtually every meeting he ever led.
adult_story:
Charlotte Elliott was born in Brighton in 1789, granddaughter of Henry Venn of the Clapham Sect and raised among evangelical believers. For her first thirty years she lived a cultured but spiritually empty life. A serious illness in 1821 unsettled her, and in May 1822 a Swiss evangelist named [[César Malan|cesar-malan]] visited her father's home and asked whether she had made her peace with God. Elliott resented the question — but it would not leave her. Days later she sought Malan out. When she asked how to come to Christ, he said simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day, marking 9 May 1822 as the birthday of her soul.

From 1829 Charlotte suffered chronic illness that largely confined her to her room for decades. She might have become bitter; instead she edited The Invalid's Hymn Book and wrote some 150 hymns — most anonymously — convinced that a bedridden saint could still serve Christ through words. In 1836 she wrote ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’ Its stanzas strip away every human condition for approaching God and rest the soul entirely on the blood of Christ. Hymnologist Kenneth Osbeck called it the hymn that had “touched more hearts and influenced more people for Christ than any other song.” Billy Graham used it at virtually every evangelistic crusade he ever led.

Charlotte Elliott brought nothing to God but her need. That was exactly what the gospel had always asked.
family_story:
Charlotte Elliott grew up in Brighton, England, but didn't trust Jesus herself. When she was thirty-two, a pastor named [[César Malan|cesar-malan]] asked her: “Have you made your peace with God?” Charlotte was upset at first — but the question wouldn't leave her. When she went back and asked how to come to God, he said: “Just come as you are.” She believed in Jesus that day.

Later, Charlotte became very ill and spent most of her life in bed. Instead of giving up, she wrote hymns. Her most famous is ‘Just As I Am’ — a song about coming to Jesus with nothing to offer but your need. Billy Graham used it to invite people to trust Christ at every meeting he ever led.

name: César Malan
source_summary: Henri Abraham César Malan (1787–1864) was a Swiss Reformed minister and hymn writer, born in Geneva. Ordained in 1810, he was transformed by Robert Haldane's 1816–1817 teaching on Romans in Geneva, which sparked the Réveil revival movement. Suspended in 1818 and formally defrocked in 1823 for preaching justification by faith alone, he founded an independent chapel, l'Église du témoignage, in his own garden and preached there for forty years. He made nine journeys to Britain and was known for personal evangelistic conversations. In May 1822 he spoke with Charlotte Elliott in Brighton and told her to 'come just as you are' — words she later transformed into the hymn 'Just As I Am, Without One Plea.'
adult_story:
César Malan was born in Geneva in 1787 and ordained as a Reformed minister in 1810. His early preaching was conventional, but in 1816 Scottish evangelist Robert Haldane came to Geneva and taught a group of young ministers through the letter to the Romans. Malan emerged from those studies gripped by justification by faith alone — and could not stop preaching it.

In 1817 a sermon on the new birth brought his first suspension. Restored, he preached the same again and was formally defrocked in 1823. He responded by founding l'Église du témoignage — the Church of Testimony — in his own garden, where he preached for forty years. He made nine journeys to Britain and was known for personal conversations about the soul's need of Christ.

One such conversation, in May 1822, changed English hymnody. At a dinner in Brighton, Malan spoke of being useful to God to a young woman named [[Charlotte Elliott|charlotte-elliott]]. She resented the remark. Days later she sought him out and asked how she might come to Christ. He told her simply: “Come just as you are.” She trusted Christ that day. Fourteen years later she turned those words into the hymn ‘Just As I Am, Without One Plea.’

Malan was defrocked for preaching that salvation is by faith alone. The words he spoke to Charlotte Elliott proved him right.
family_story:
César Malan was a pastor in Geneva, Switzerland, who got into serious trouble for telling the truth. He believed people are saved not by being good, but by trusting in Jesus alone — and he preached this so boldly that church leaders fired him.

Instead of giving up, Malan built a little chapel in his garden and kept preaching for the rest of his life.

One day in England in 1822, he met a young woman named [[Charlotte Elliott|charlotte-elliott]]. She asked how she could come to God. He said: “Just come as you are.” She trusted Jesus that day — and fourteen years later she wrote the hymn ‘Just As I Am,’ sung by millions ever since.
